subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — Census Bureau Oversight and Congressional Investigation
stimulus:
On March 14, 2019, while testifying before a congressional committee, Secretary of Commerce Wilbur Ross said that "The Department of Commerce is fully committed to administering as complete and accurate a decennial census as we can." Yet, the Census Bureau (a bureaucratic agency that is part of the Department of Commerce) had not met its deadlines and had failed to submit key documents to the House of Representatives Committee on Oversight and Reform at the end of 2020.

Committee Chairperson Carolyn B. Maloney grew concerned after holding a town hall meeting and after reading a New York Times report that listed the census's many issues. In December 2020, she sent a letter to Secretary Ross, urging him to provide documents and to explain the Census Bureau's position. Maloney stressed that the secretary had not communicated any of the problems listed in the Times report to the committee.

The Constitution mandates a census every ten years in a manner directed by Congress. Chairperson Maloney argued that Secretary Ross, by not being transparent with the committee, was stopping Congress from fulfilling its constitutional duty.

parts.prompt: Describe the implied power of Congress being exercised in the scenario.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Describes the implied power of oversight. Accept: Congress is reviewing the actions of the bureaucracy; Congress is using oversight by requesting information from the bureaucracy; the power of oversight is being exercised when Secretary Ross testifies before the House committee.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'oversight,' 'power of oversight,' or 'implied power of investigation/oversight.' The response must describe what the power involves in the scenario.

parts.prompt: Explain how the media can influence the use of the power described in part A.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains how media influences congressional oversight. Accept: the media informs voters of an issue, who could then urge Congress to investigate the bureaucracy; Congress can learn about the Census's problems through the media (as in this scenario — Maloney read the New York Times report) and use its oversight powers to resolve them.
parts.scoring_notes: The scenario itself provides a concrete example — Chairperson Maloney acted after reading a New York Times report. Accept any accurate explanation of how media triggers or amplifies congressional oversight.

parts.prompt: Explain how the chairperson's actions in the scenario might influence someone engaged in retrospective voting.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains the connection between the chairperson's actions and retrospective voting. Accept: the retrospective voter model predicts that voters who approve of Maloney's oversight actions will be more likely to vote for her in the next election; voters who disagree with Maloney's decision may vote against her in the future; a citizen may choose to vote for Maloney's party in an upcoming congressional election based on her behavior; voters might choose to not vote for the incumbent president because they were dissatisfied with the Commerce Secretary's performance.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect the chairperson's actions to the retrospective voting model — voters evaluating past performance when deciding how to vote. Accept any plausible connection.
